perturbation.original_text: SutroVax will pay such invoices within [***] days of receipt of invoice (including all required documentation) by SutroVax.
perturbation.changed_text: SutroVax will pay such invoices within 60 days of receipt of invoice (including all required documentation) by SutroVax.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to a fixed 60 days regardless of the agreed-upon timeframe in the contract can introduce payment delays that could contradict California's prompt payment laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 2751 requires employers to have written contracts with employees, and this contract dictates payment terms. Unduly long or uncertain payment terms could be challenged under California law, especially if they deviate significantly from industry standards or create undue hardship for the supplier. If the original timeframe was shorter than 60 days, this edit would potentially violate the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 2751 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 3. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. The Contract of Employment [2750 - 2755] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 2751. (a) Whenever an employer enters into a contract of employment with an employee for services to be rendered within this state and the contemplated method of payment of the employee involves commissions, the contract shall be in writing and shall set forth the method by which the commissions shall be computed and paid. (b) The employer shall give a signed copy of the contract to every employee who is a party thereto and shall obtain a signed receipt for the contract from each employee. In the case of a contract that expires and where the parties nevertheless continue to work under the terms of the expired contract, the contract terms are presumed to remain in full force and effect until the contract is superseded or employment is terminated by either party. (c) As used in this section, “commissions” has the meaning set forth in Section 204.1. For purposes of this section only, “commission” does not include any of the following: (1) Short-term productivity bonuses
perturbation.justification: The change from '[***] days' to '60 days' creates a potential legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this fixed 60-day term may introduce payment delays that contradict California's prompt payment laws. The Law Explanation also states that unduly long or uncertain payment terms could be challenged under California law, especially if the original timeframe was shorter than 60 days. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Labor Code Section 2751(a), requires written contracts with employees to set forth the method by which commissions shall be computed and paid. Although this snippet focuses on commission payments, the broader principle it establishes – that payment terms must be clearly defined in writing – supports the argument that a change to a potentially longer payment timeframe could be problematic if it violates prompt payment standards or deviates from standard practices.

perturbation.original_text: Notices will be deemed delivered on the earliest of transmission by facsimile, actual receipt or [***] days after mailing as described herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Notices will be deemed delivered only upon actual receipt, regardless of transmission method or mailing date.
perturbation.explanation: Eliminating the 'transmission by facsimile or mailing date' clauses and requiring 'actual receipt' for notice introduces a potential delay or uncertainty in when a notice is considered delivered, especially in legal contexts. This may conflict with established legal standards regarding notification periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP § 1013 specifies how service by mail is considered complete and extends certain deadlines based on mailing date. Requiring actual receipt disregards the legally defined timeline for service by mail, potentially conflicting with legal proceedings where timely notice is critical.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, which requires 'actual receipt' of notices, directly contradicts CCP § 1013(a) as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1. CCP § 1013(a) states that 'Service is complete at the time of the deposit' in the mail, provided specific conditions are met (sealed envelope, postage paid, proper address). The modified text eliminates this legally recognized method of service completion, replacing it with a requirement of actual receipt, regardless of mailing date. This fundamentally undermines the legal provision that deems service complete upon mailing, thus creating a significant contradiction and potentially impacting legal timelines.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall provide the other with written notice at least [***] days prior to the cancellation, non renewal or material change in such insurance.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall endeavor to provide the other with written notice prior to the cancellation, non renewal or material change in such insurance, but is not obligated to do so.
perturbation.explanation: Weakening the obligation to provide notice regarding insurance changes to a mere 'endeavor' and removing a concrete timeframe introduces significant risk. Failure to provide adequate notice of insurance changes may violate insurance regulations or create contractual issues.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Insurance Code § 678.1 dictates notice requirements for policy cancellations or non-renewals. While this section focuses on insurers, the principle of providing timely notice is fundamental in insurance law. Removing the 'at least [***] days' written notice' clause can expose a party to liability if lack of notice causes damages that could have been avoided with the required notice.

GENERAL RULES GOVERNING INSURANCE [100 - 1879.8] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) PART 1. THE CONTRACT [100 - 679.75] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) CHAPTER 11. Cancellation and Failure to Renew Certain Property Insurance [675 - 679.7] ( Chapter 11 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 313. ) 678.1. (a) This section applies only to policies of insurance of commercial insurance that are subject to Sections 675.5 and 676.6. (b) A notice of nonrenewal shall be in writing and shall be delivered or mailed to the producer of record and to the named insured at the mailing address shown on the policy. The time periods and procedures in subdivision (a) of Section 1013 of the Code of Civil Procedure shall be applicable if the notice is mailed. (c) An insurer, at least 60 days, but not more than 120 days, in advance of the end of the policy period, shall give notice of nonrenewal, and the reasons for the nonrenewal, if the insurer intends not to renew the policy, or to condition renewal upon reduction of limits, elimination of coverages, increase in deductibles, or increase of more than 25 percent in the rate upon which the premium is based. (d) If an insurer fails to give timely notice required by subdivision (c), the policy of
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates written notice 'at least [***] days prior' to insurance changes, while the changed text weakens this to 'endeavor to provide...but is not obligated to do so.' This directly contradicts the principle of providing timely notice, which, according to the Law Explanation, is fundamental in insurance law and necessary to avoid liability. California Insurance Code § 678.1, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, mandates specific notice periods for nonrenewal in commercial insurance policies. While the snippet focuses on nonrenewal and insurer obligations, it establishes the legal importance of timely notice in insurance contexts. By removing the mandatory notice period, the changed text creates a scenario where a party might not provide sufficient notice, potentially leading to damages and legal disputes that timely notice would have prevented, thus creating a legal contradiction as defined by the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' perturbation type.
